My distributor gave me a $10k advance. When do I recoup? [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution"}]

Recoupment analysis on advances is Finance's domain — I handle the technical distribution pipeline. Routing to Agent Zero to loop in Finance. On my end, I can confirm your release metadata and royalty routing are set up correctly so every stream is tracked and counted toward that recoupment.

My distributor is offering me an exclusive distribution deal with a higher commission but better editorial support. Should I go exclusive? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"label-deal","stage":"developing","genre":"pop"}}]

For someone building toward a label deal, this decision plays differently. An exclusive distribution relationship with the right distributor can actually accelerate your path — if the terms are right.

**Why exclusivity can help a label-seeking artist:**
- Major distributors with exclusive deals often have direct relationships with label A&Rs. Being on their radar is valuable.
- Better editorial support means higher visibility on DSPs, which means better streaming numbers, which means stronger bargaining position when labels come calling.
- An exclusive deal signals to the industry that a distributor is investing in you — that's a form of validation.

**But negotiate these terms:**
- Short term: 1 year maximum with an option for year 2. Don't lock yourself in for 3 years — if a label deal materializes, you need to be free to sign.
- Exit clause: If you sign a label deal, the distribution agreement must release you without a holdback penalty.
- Catalog reversion: When the deal ends, your catalog should be fully portable within 30 days.
- The commission should be 15% maximum for an exclusive — anything higher is taking advantage.

**The key question:** Does this distributor have a track record of artists going from their roster to label deals? Ask for specific examples. If they can name 3 artists who graduated from distribution to label deals through their editorial support, that's real. If they can't, the exclusivity isn't earning its premium.

I'm releasing a cover of a popular song through DistroKid. I need to pay for the mechanical license through Harry Fox. Can you handle that? [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true}]

Processing the mechanical license payment for your cover release. Running `payment_gate` to initiate payment to Harry Fox Agency (HFA) via the Songfile system.

Typical cost for cover mechanical licenses via DistroKid: DistroKid's 'Help Cover Song' service: $12/year per cover (recommended — they handle HFA paperwork). Direct HFA Songfile: ~$15-$21 for a 2-year digital license depending on song length.

I'm also running `create_music_metadata` to properly flag this release as a cover in your metadata — the original songwriter must be credited in the mechanical license field, and your distributor needs to know the composition ownership is NOT yours (only the master recording is).

Once payment is confirmed, you'll receive the license certificate. Keep this on file — it's your legal protection for distributing this cover commercially.

Does my metadata meet Apple Music's requirements? [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true}]

Running Apple Music metadata compliance check... Key requirements: Artist name capitalization (title case), no ALL CAPS, no special characters in track titles, ISRC on every track, explicit flag (yes/no/clean version available), genre must match Apple taxonomy. Result: [PASS/FAIL details from QC tool].

What metadata actually matters when I upload a release? I usually just fill in title, artist name, and genre. [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true}]

Metadata is the invisible engine behind discoverability. DSP algorithms use it to decide who hears your music.

Essential: Track title (clean formatting, no ALL CAPS). Contributing artists (full names for all featured/primary). ISRC code (unique per recording, never reuse across versions). UPC/EAN (release level). Genre AND sub-genre (be specific — 'Hip-Hop/Lo-fi Hip Hop' beats just 'Hip-Hop'). Release date (set 3-4 weeks out for playlist pitching). Copyright info.

Critical for Discovery: Mood tags (Happy, Melancholic, Energetic, Chill — DSPs use these for mood playlists). BPM (workout playlists, DJ tools). Key (mood algorithms). Lyrics (full lyrics improve search on Apple Music and Spotify). Songwriter/Producer credits (feeds PRO matching). Language. Explicit flag (accurate labeling prevents editorial playlist exclusion).

Power Moves: ISNI/IPI numbers to link identity across databases. Pre-save links via Feature.fm.

Common mistakes: Listing producer as 'featured artist' instead of in credits. Different artist name spellings across releases (fragments your profile). Wrong auto-detected BPM. Incorrect territory rights.

Treat metadata as part of release prep — same importance as mixing and mastering.

Can you analyze my track and generate the metadata automatically? [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true,"hasAudio":true}]

Running AI metadata generation from your audio file — create_music_metadata. Analyzing: BPM, key, energy, genre classification, mood descriptors, instrument detection. Output: Spotify/Apple-compliant metadata fields including genre tags, mood, and DSP optimization data. Review and confirm before submission — AI classification should always be human-verified.

I'm trying to decide between DistroKid, TuneCore, CD Baby, AWAL, and Stem. What should I go with as a mid-level indie doing 200k streams/month? [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true}]

Here's a 2026 comparison at your level (200k streams/month):

DistroKid ($22.99/year, unlimited releases): Best for high output. Keep 100% of royalties. Does NOT register publishing — handle your own PRO and admin. If you leave, music comes down unless you pay for Leave a Legacy ($49/track).

TuneCore ($9.99-$29.99/single, $49.99/album/year): Clean dashboard and YouTube monetization. Annual renewal fees PER RELEASE add up — 20 singles = $200-$600/year. Expensive for large catalogs.

CD Baby ($9.95/single, $29.95/album one-time): Pay once and forget. Offers sync licensing pipeline via CD Baby Sync. Takes 9% commission on all revenue — at 200k streams/month (~$700-$800), that's $63-$72/month.

AWAL (application only, 15% commission): Playlist pitching support and analytics without a label deal. 15% of your ~$9,000/year streaming = $1,350/year to AWAL. Selective — owned by Sony.

Stem (commission-based, ~5-10%): Transparent split payments to collaborators and lower commission than AWAL. Smaller DSP footprint in some territories.

My recommendation: DistroKid for cost efficiency, paired with Songtrust or Sentric for publishing admin. At your trajectory, you'll likely move to a proper indie distribution deal (Orchard, Believe, Empire) within 12-18 months.

Is my track ready for distribution? I want to make sure everything meets the Golden Standard. [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true}]

Running Golden File verification: (1) Audio: 44.1kHz/16-bit WAV or higher — no spectral fraud. (2) Metadata: ISRC, UPC, artist, title, genre, explicit flag, split data. (3) Tax: all payees W-9/W-8BEN certified. (4) Rights: no uncleared samples flagged. Calling verify_metadata_golden and run_audio_qc now. Any FAIL halts the pipeline.

What is Merlin and am I compliant? [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution"}]

Merlin is the global rights agency for independent labels — members negotiate better DSP licensing rates than standard aggregator deals. Running Merlin compliance check... check_merlin_status. If you qualify (typically $250k+ annual streaming revenue threshold), Merlin membership can increase your per-stream rates by 10-15% on participating DSPs (Spotify, Deezer, Pandora).

Store my TuneCore login so you can check the status of my releases automatically without asking me every time. [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true}]

Storing your TuneCore credentials securely via `credential_vault`. Your login is encrypted and stored — I'll retrieve it automatically when checking release statuses, delivery reports, or earnings statements from TuneCore, so you won't be prompted each time.

With stored credentials, I can proactively alert you to: release delivery failures, rejected releases (and the specific rejection reason), stores that haven't picked up your release after 72 hours, and payment processing confirmations.
